sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
Now, I have to mention that I was the oldest granddaughter.
And I know my family was very proud of me, to the point that when it came to go to-- to gymnasium, gymnasium was somehow a little more important than the high school here.
Because high school, everybody goes to high school.
There you had to pay.
And you had to pay exams.
So the times already were harsh.
You know, you already felt the anti-Semitism.
You want me to talk about this at this time?
Yes.
At this time, this in my town, this Debica, since you know the war, it was the pressure.
We heard about Germany and Hitler.
It was in the air, something was-- it was going bad.
The anti-Semitism was rising in Poland.
My town belonged to a-- they created a industrial or somehow a development triangle that a few towns belonged.
There was a factory, a tire factory.
They were supposed to rebuild this to bring, to induce to industrialize.
You know, because they were backward in Poland.
You know, like Germany was already arming and doing.
There was a big factory.
So this town was becoming a little more important.
It had a link too with the railroad to Warsaw, to Krakow, to Lwow.
And so I remembered that my father's brother from [INAUDIBLE], he came, and there was a family-- a family reunion family.
Get together, conference.
That instead of me, since that time that I think was my decision too, when you are going to gymnasium, you are getting an academic education, right?
But then they opened-- there was a business, a commercial gymnasium in Poland.
It was a very good education, right.
Maybe they didn't stress so much, you know, literature or something, but you are getting a basic good education, and also the business subject.
I remember now many of the things when I was going to FIT, about fabrics and things, you know I remember from before the war.
And so the decision was made that it's-- I should go to a professional-- I've got to have a profession.
Because you have education, you can always go back on your academic education.
That's how it was important.
And that I didn't realize, but I was a big-- you know, their pride.
And so I started going to this, and you have to pay for it.
We're getting up in the-- the morning in the snow, and to school.
It was a very good school.
But I already felt the pressures of, you know, anti-Semitism.
I think there were new professors coming from other towns.
There was the principal was a woman, a very nice woman from Lwow.
You know, she was the principal.
But I wasn't called as much to something, and it wasn't this easy somehow.
I don't know if I slipped, or maybe it was more difficult.
But it was-- I started to feel I didn't feel it as a child that I was Jewish.
Yes, let's say-- let's say in Poland, you know, it was a very militaristic, you know, upbringing.
They were always you know, right wing, I guess, you call it, right?
They're always glorifying their heroes, Napoleon.
And-- and there was also in the high school, we were getting education, military education kind of.
And this was in Polish.
It was like preparation, military preparation.
It was a woman, and they came and they, you know, had to wear uniforms, probably something like this Hitlerjugend I don't know.
It wasn't the same.
And it was in the gymnasiums.
I remember that came an instructor, a woman in the uniform.
And she was something, and I remember, although you know, you-- you thought that it's-- it's shown on your face, right, that you were Jewish.
And there was something.
And I asked, are we allowed too, to join this?
She says, why not?
Because I felt maybe the Jews are not allowed anymore, right.
And this was a kind of a route, really military.
But I guess parading, and maybe they-- I don't remember shooting, but something, you know, a military type of drilling.
And, you know, it was in the afternoons.
And I remember this, when I asked this instructor, are we allowed too?
Can we join too?
Because the Jews didn't-- they weren't allowed even to become scouts.
You know, there were there boy scouts, girl scouts, but no-- no-- no Jews.
Was that always the case?
Yes.
Yes.
They were not allowed.
The Jews at the time, the Jews were not allowed in the universities, not in mine and in my husband's town.
They had to sit in the back.
Yeah, maybe she didn't recognize me.
But the fact is that I thought it's written on my face that I am Jewish, right?
You already felt this, and it was very oppressive.
And I started telling you about this industrialization.
You know, if this is the right word.
Then came a lot of people to build these new factories.
There was one with-- it's still there, I've heard.
I think it was bought off now by Michelin, the tire factory.
A lot of people came from Posen, from West Poland.
And they must have been probably pro-German.
It was bordering with Germany.
And then and the anti-- anti-Semitism, you know, people were walking around dressed in their traditional-- not everybody, not my father or my family.
But there were some.
And they were hunting them, and-- and calling names, and you know, they're saying something like-- we never had a Kristall night, you know.
It wasn't so bad.
But you felt it.
And it was-- it was oppressive.
While I was going to this gymnasium, I felt this pressure of being the second-class citizens and being-- it-- it was-- really depressing.
Maybe other people didn't, others I know my-- some were survivors from my town.
And they always talk about their town.
Maybe they had a happier childhood, where some went back and they wanted to reclaim something, and-- and I meet somebody here.
And he always says, oh, you remember this and this, you remember this one.
And I said, why do you always talk about this Debica?
I want to divorce, I wanted to divorce myself from this.
So you began to see changes in Debica?
Yes, yes, yes.
Yes, and I also, my personal feeling, I resented this traditional background, you know?
I wanted to assimilate.
Well, lots of people are assimilating.
You know, it didn't help them later.
But that's what I, you know, I would have preferred my family to be assimilated.
It was a very strong feeling in me.
Somehow-- I-- I don't know-- I kept my-- my--
Yes.
Yes, this was the--
Yes, it was September 1st, yes.
Yes, when we heard it was going already, it was going on, the preparations.
There were-- there was this general, the head of the-- you see when I am getting nervous I forget the names.
[INAUDIBLE], I remember he was.
And there were these speeches on the radio.
And we not going to give up a button from our things, and all this big talk.
And I remember the oppressive feeling, the anxiety.
Right, the anxiety?
Where were you that day?
I was in Debica.
And then I was-- I remember when this came.
It was in Debica.
And maybe I went to my grandparents too.
Because I-- I know it came.
And we had neighbors, next-door neighbors.
You know, they were very close, my girlfriend.
Yes.
We had Polish neighbors.
We had Jewish neighbors, a lot of Jewish neighbors.
The doors were open.
I have to stress that what's missing here, what I remember, I had the support system in the neighborhood.
A community.
When we get word we were getting guests, right, and there was not enough room to-- for them to sleep, I was going to the neighbors to sleep.
After school, I was coming.
I was stepping to one neighbor, getting a cookie or milk, and then going.
You know, it was the doors were open, you know.
You didn't ring the bell.
You were just going in, you know, and families.
And you belonged.
But I have the fear, I always felt this outsider.
I am not a psychologist, probably from my separation from the early-- from my family or my mother, or maybe I have to say, maybe I was precautious.
I-- because, I know somebody from my town, you know, that went back twice to Poland.
I never wanted to go back.
I don't-- I don't care what is there.
Yes.
Yeah, so when the war broke out, now I remember.
You know, I forgot about this altogether.
We-- everybody was running away.
My girlfriend, right next door, the whole family went east.
They left everything, and they ran east.
A lot of people were running away, because they're invading.
You know, we were not at the border, right?
It probably took two, three weeks.
They went east to Lemberg.
They were annexed by the Russians, right?
And they survived in Siberia.
Did you see them leaving?
Yes.
Yes, I remember.
We had the kind-- we were sitting in the front of the house.
There was such a big thing for the water, rainwater.
We had hard water, right?
We had-- we collected rainwater in something.
It was covered with something.
They were sitting there, and at night before, and then the second-- then they all run away.
And we run away to Pruszkow.
I remember now that everything-- there were no cars.
Maybe there was somebody on the car with wagons and horses, right?
So my family ran away.
A-- a village to some peasants.
I remember this.
I think it was Pruszkow.
P-R-U-S-Z-K-O-W, but I don't remember if this is it.
With the wagon and the children, and-- everything.
Not furniture, we took clothing.
We took clothing and possessions for a few days.
Because-- we really ran away from the bombardment.
Because they were-- yes, we heard finally the bombs were falling somewhere in other streets.
So people would run away.
And then after a few days, we came back.
I remember I had a fiddle, you know, that I was given, although I don't have any musical talents.
And something was missing.
And then I don't know exactly how it was, but I went back.
Because it didn't start, the oppression, everything, didn't start right away.
I went back to my home, and then maybe for the weekend I went to my grandmother, the one my father's, the one that I-- I liked.
And there I remember I was sitting in the store with the-- everything shut, looking through the window out.
And I saw the Germans arriving in the street.
You know, this was-- there was a big square where they lived, you know, with the stores.
It was like this from the medieval times.
It was the town square, where the stores were, where the-- the peasants were coming once a week to sell their vegetables and things.
And that's-- I remember the Germans.
And I saw them.
They came on the motorcycles with-- with the-- how do you call these things?
There was a motorcycle, and next to it.
With a slide car.
With a side car, yes.
I remember this moment.
They were coming.
We were sitting through-- looking through the window.
And there was the road where these buses were-- were going, and horses.
And then they had the horse.
The whole square was, you know, the horses and these, and this is, you know.
This is how I saw them coming.
And it was a very scary--
Yes.
No, no, no, no.
I don't remember.
I was told, I never saw these things.
I mean they were told when they came they burned the locals synagogue, and the man that was taking-- the caretaker of the synagogue, and some other people.
They put them in, and they burned the synagogue with them.
And it was somehow unbelievable, you know.
We-- I remember who it was, the caretaker I think.
I don't know if he was alone.
There were other people.
They locked them to the synagogue, and they-- they burned them.
Then they were-- I didn't see it.
They were cutting off the beards.
Then I remember myself back home, right?
Because it was like an hour's bus ride-- I don't know how long.
I was home.
We still had our maid, and the kitchen, it was in the-- it was already later.
It was later.
I don't know exactly.
It must have been already winter.
It was cold.
And the German soldiers came.
There was still not fraternizing or something we were going to.
Soldiers came in, and two soldiers-- they were big husky something.
You didn't see such people in Poland.
They were very tall and strong.
And they were coming.
They asked if they could warm up their rations, the food.
They had this-- what is the name of the [NON-ENGLISH], the military--
The K-rations, whatever they had, soup I think.
They had soup.
They were much more-- and they asked [NON-ENGLISH].
Their stoves were wood-burning stoves, or tile stove, a big stove, with-- like the restaurants have these tops, you, know something with the wood burning.
And we didn't know what was going to be.
And I remember, I don't know.
I spoke German.
I had two years of German in this high school, right?
And it was similar to-- to Yiddish.
I mentioned that something about the Jews, they ask.
I said, but we are Jewish.
I mean at the time, we didn't know that-- how-- and they were stunned.
This is a Jewish home.
But it's so clean.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everything was-- they were really stunned.
The guys, some they were told that the Jews were probably it's with the lice and with the dirt, and with this propaganda, the way they were depicted.
And they were stunned.
And they were coming, and they ate there, and they had conversations.
Then there was another German, maybe a sergeant from Vienna.
And he was coming, and visiting in the evening.
And he was bringing something.
This was in the beginning.
And I remember, you know, like you recall certain things that this soldier, when I told him.
Yeah, but we are-- we are Jewish.
You are Jewish?
This is Jewish home?
This is a Jewish house?
They couldn't believe it.
They continued to come back.
I mean they were probably moving somewhere else.
But then there was another guy from Vienna.
I know he had this Viennese accent that knew Jews.
He was a businessman from Vienna.
And he was coming and visiting, having tea.
And he talked German.
And I didn't mention my father run away too.
The men were running away east, and they're leaving their families behind.
Because somehow, I don't know why, the feeling was that the men will be killed, or the men will be persecuted.
So my father left.
And we were home, my stepmother, with the four children.
There was still a maid.
She still had the store.
And now that I am, you know, when I grew up, you know, I-- I realize now when you are a teenager, right, you see things differently.
She was the-- somehow an outsider.
But she was-- she was 31 years old, like my mother.
And we-- I never did anything at home.
I mean I didn't-- I wasn't getting any chores to do.
But sometimes, I mean, this was before the war, I was really spoiled.
You know, when came the holidays, let's say the Easter holidays and people we are very busy in the store, because people were painting.
And so she was helping out, and I was always reading, reading and studying.
And it never occurred to me to go and help with the dishes, or if there was-- the maid wasn't-- you know, they were changing help.
No, this was before the war.
I just remembered now the relationship with the stepmother.
Right, the mother would tell you-- Helen, you go do this and this, right?
But she wouldn't.
She expected me to do it.
I didn't do it.
And then my father interfered, why I didn't do it.
Then I had to kiss her hand and apologize.
That I didn't help with the dishes.
Then this-- the war has started, I myself started doing things, mending and I don't know.
And I remember that I felt that she was-- I was scrubbing the kitchen floor.
It was a wooden floor.
And every week it had to be scrubbed with-- and dry, and it was the cleanliness was important.
You know, everything was, you know, like-- the bad things and the carpet was aired.
You know, and there were no dryers, you know.
And so I started-- I remember this that I thought I will scrub the floor, because she's too old.
It's too hard for her.
She was 31.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes, yes.
My father came back too-- later.
Because we are corresponding with him.
But then that's what people probably are not-- everybody knows about the concentration camps, right.
They were 1942.
This was 1939, beginning of 1940, right?
Yes, I don't know exactly when it started.
But the first thing, they came and when we had to give up all the furs.
And people were so scared.
Everybody was giving away.
Nobody somehow can I say something, what my husband told me that he read somewhere.
That let's say the Israelis, right, looked down at the survivors, that why didn't they resist, right?
Why didn't they resist?
But I remember, we were always the second-class citizens in Poland, always being put down, and-- and called names by the Poles.
We had no rights, let's say, in the universities.
They were sitting in the back.
They were not allowed to some universities.
I know my husband's cousins and his father-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my husband's father had a PhD in chemistry before the war.
And he studied in-- in Prague.
And he had another-- his mother had a the cousin, a doctor, a surgeon who studied in Prague too, not in Poland, because they were not allowed.
It was not in the character.
Not possible, because I have to stress this fact that I just cannot get used to this violence here and the crime, and this something.
So we were not well-- you know, there were no luxuries.
There were no washing machines.
There were no something.
But what it was there, it was-- there was really no crime, maybe in Warsaw.
And the Jews, nobody would-- you know, never heard of a Jew striking another one.
This atmosphere--
The first thing they came--
The furs, yes.
I-- and slowly, let's say there was snow.
So every family had to send a member of the family to shovel snow.
And I remember myself had to go and-- you know, they didn't have to-- this was Poland, right?
The sidewalks-- it's a sidewalk, right?
With-- where the stores, sidewalks were covered with ice.
And I was-- you know, had such had such this-- oh, I forgot.
My Fred would know the name of this thing that you hacked the ice.
An ice chopper.
An ice chopper, or ice something.
And I was there, and me and some other young, you know, chopping.
And I remember my girlfriend, a Polish girlfriend, her parents were teachers.
And she was-- and she was my girlfriend.
I was the big-- you know, keeping my nose up, because I was so good in school.
And she passed by, and it was so degrading.
You know, I have to explain that also Poland had a different, you know, it was degrading somehow to do such a menial--
--physical labor.
Which was I think terrible.
Because when I came here, and I stayed with my relatives, the little boys came to shovel snow.
And they were paid for it.
And it was nothing to it.
Right?
We're Jews, yes.
And then later on, I was sent to-- it was 30 below, and I was sent to the-- you know, they always transported the soldiers or whatever equipment in the fields.
I was dressed up.
It was very cold.
It was windy, and the snow was blowing.
I was all dressed up.
And I had to shovel snow, my brother too.
But I remember what I had to do.
And then I came-- I had a piece of a sandwich with marmalade, bread with marmalade, a sandwich.
I couldn't eat it.
It was all frozen in the packet.
You know, but this is-- this was my first recollections.
Of course, later, it was much worse, with the camps.
But that was already this beginning, rations.
I have to-- to mention something else.
OK.
OK.
So--
